name: Alto-Minhoto Portuguese

allophone_rules.notes: Northern betacism (Cintra's trait 1, all-Northern): the /v/ ~ /b/ phonological opposition is lost and both merge into a single phoneme /b/ (vinho -> [ˈbiɲu], vaca -> [ˈbakɐ], estava -> [ʃˈtabɐ]). Cintra (1971:87): 'o desaparecimento da oposição fonológica entre os fonemas /v/ e /b/ e a sua fusão num fonema único /b/, realizado ora como oclusiva, ora como fricativa (ou espirante) b ou β' — the merged phoneme surfaces either as the stop [b] or the spirant [β], the [β] variant recorded in the `allophones` inventory. Modelled as a post-lexical phoneme-side rewrite so the merger applies uniformly however /v/ arises from the inherited grapheme map. Attested in the CLUP Alto-Minho gold (vou -> [bow], ver -> [beɾ]).

notes: Alto-Minhoto Portuguese of the Alto Minho (Viana do Castelo, Ponte de Lima). Cintra (1971:93) groups Alto-Minho TOGETHER WITH Trás-os-Montes (pt-PT-x-trasosmontes) as a single 'grupo transmontano-alto-minhoto', the conservative Northern varieties that alone retain the medieval FOUR-SIBILANT system; the two specs share the same sibilant deltas. A DELTA over standard (Lisbon-type) pt-PT: inherits the whole standard EP system (unstressed vowel reduction, dark coda /l/, sandhi) via graphemes_base / allophones_base and declares only the archaic-Northern deltas. MODELLED FEATURES: (1) FOUR-SIBILANT SYSTEM (Cintra trait 2, his single most diagnostic North/South isogloss) — the medieval apico-alveolar vs (pre)dorsodental contrast, lost everywhere else, is kept: the grapheme series ⟨s⟩/⟨ss⟩ realises the voiceless APICO-ALVEOLAR [s̺] (word-initial, word-final, and intervocalic ⟨ss⟩) with its voiced intervocalic-⟨s⟩ counterpart [z̺], contrasting with the LAMINAL (predorsodental) [s] of ⟨c⟩(before e/i)/⟨ç⟩ and [z] of ⟨z⟩ (inherited unchanged from pt-PT, left unmarked — the Mirandese/mwl notation convention). So the minimal pairs Cintra cites stay distinct: passo [ˈpas̺u] vs paço [ˈpasu], coser [kuˈz̺eɾ] vs cozer [kuˈzeɾ]. Cintra (1971:93): 'a existência de um sistema de quatro sibilantes — [s̺] e [z̺] ápicoalveolares (correspondentes aos grafemas s e ss), característicos de todos os falares setentrionais, opondo-se a e convivendo com o [s] e o [z] predorsodentais (correspondentes aos grafemas ce,i, ç e z)'; note 29 fixes the positions: '[s̺] ápico-alveolar (correspondente a s inicial e final e ss interior)'. Álvarez Pérez (2014:37, §4) confirms this conservative four-sibilant system is precisely the 'Transmontanos and Alto-Minhotos' group and quantifies it from the ALEPG. NOTATION: Cintra's own term is apico-alveolar / 'reverso'; his [s̺] [z̺] are commonly rendered [ʂ] [ʐ] in modern IPA (the 's beirão'/'reverso' is acoustically retroflex-like), recorded as the surface variant in `allophones`; the [s̺]/[z̺] symbols are Cintra's own and are used as the default. LIMIT: preconsonantal coda ⟨s⟩ (festa, gosto) keeps the inherited pan-EP chiado [ʃ] — Cintra's note 29 enumerates the [s̺] positions as 's inicial e final e ss interior' only, so the preconsonantal palatalisation, a separate process, is left inherited rather than overridden. (2) ARCHAIC ⟨ch⟩ AFFRICATE (Cintra trait 3) — the syllable-onset /tʃ/–/ʃ/ contrast is preserved: ⟨ch⟩ = [tʃ] (chave -> [ˈtʃabɨ]) vs ⟨x⟩ = [ʃ], not merged as in standard EP. Cintra (1971:87): 'a permanência da distinção fonológica em posição inicial de sílaba entre o fonema /tʃ/, representado pelo grafema ch e o fonema /ʃ/ representado pelo grafema x'. Modelled as an inherited-map grapheme delta. Porto's tonic-close-vowel diphthongisation ([e]->[je], [o]->[wo]) is a Baixo-Minho/Douro-Litoral (baixo-minhoto-duriense) feature and is deliberately NOT applied to the transmontano-alto-minhoto group (Cintra 1971:93).

ancestors.notes: Standard (Lisbon-type) European Portuguese; Alto-Minhoto diverges by Northern betacism (v->b), the conservative four-sibilant system (apico-alveolar ⟨s,ss⟩ vs laminal ⟨c,ç,z⟩), and the archaic ⟨ch⟩ affricate [tʃ].

sources.author: Cintra, Luís F. Lindley
sources.title: Nova proposta de classificação dos dialectos galego-portugueses
sources.publisher: Boletim de Filologia (Centro de Estudos Filológicos, Lisboa), vol. 22, pp. 81-116
sources.notes: Foundational classification of the Galician-Portuguese dialect space by maximally diagnostic phonetic traits. Trait 1 (betacism, p.87): 'o desaparecimento da oposição fonológica entre os fonemas /v/ e /b/ e a sua fusão num fonema único /b/, realizado ora como oclusiva, ora como fricativa (ou espirante) b ou β'. Trait 2 (apico-alveolar sibilant, p.87), his PRIMARY North/South isogloss: '/s/ ... e /z/, como fricativas ápico-alveolares'. Trait 3 (ch affricate, p.87): 'a permanência da distinção fonológica em posição inicial de sílaba entre o fonema /tʃ/, representado pelo grafema ch e o fonema /ʃ/ representado pelo grafema x'. p.93 groups Alto-Minho with Trás-os-Montes as the four-sibilant 'grupo transmontano-alto-minhoto': 'um sistema de quatro sibilantes — [s̺] e [z̺] ápicoalveolares (correspondentes aos grafemas s e ss) ... opondo-se ... ao [s] e o [z] predorsodentais (correspondentes aos grafemas ce,i, ç e z)'. Note 29 (footnote to the p.93 discussion): '[s̺] ápico-alveolar (correspondente a s inicial e final e ss interior) ... [z̺] ápicoalveolar (correspondente ao s intervocálico)'; names the series 'ápicoalveolares ou reversos'. Republished in Cintra, Estudos de Dialectologia Portuguesa, Sá da Costa, 1983, pp. 117-163.

sources.author: Álvarez Pérez, Xosé Afonso
sources.title: European Portuguese dialectal features: a comparison with Cintra's proposal
sources.notes: §4 (Sibilants, pp.36-38): documents, from the Atlas Linguístico-Etnográfico de Portugal e da Galiza (ALEPG) data, that the conservative four-sibilant system 'is found in northern and northwestern Portugal, in the group of dialects that Cintra called Transmontanos and Alto-Minhotos'. Sets out the grapheme/etymology mapping used here: s- initial and -ss- -> apico-alveolar [s̺]; intervocalic -s- -> [z̺]; ⟨c⟩(before e/i)/⟨ç⟩ -> laminal [s]; ⟨z⟩ -> laminal [z]. Quantitative dialectometric corroboration of Cintra's isogloss.

sources.author: Mateus, M.H.M. & d'Andrade, E.
sources.title: The Phonology of Portuguese
sources.notes: Reference EP phonology for the inherited base processes (unstressed vowel reduction, dark coda /l/) that Alto-Minhoto inherits unchanged from pt-PT.

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam).
